speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 12, 1996. I will be interviewing Felix Pierson. The interviewer is Tina Tito. We are in Monticello, New York in the United States. The language will be English. Today's August 12, 1996. I am Tina Tito, the interviewer. I will be interviewing Felix Pierson. Mr. Pierson, can you tell me your full name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. It's Felix-- F-E-L-I-X-- Pierson, P as in pull, I-E-R-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth-- your full name at birth. It was Feiwel-- F-E-I-W-E-L-- Piernakarz. It's P-I-E-R-N-I-K-A-R-Z. Now you know why I changed it. Your date of birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: February 17, '26.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city of Lask-- L-A-S-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland, right. That's near Lodz. I tease my friend. I tell them that Lodz was near Lask. But that city was organized in 1492, the same year that America was discovered. So it was a very old city that had Jews from the very beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how long your family lived in Lask?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never came up. But as far as I know, probably forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your grandparents, as far as you know and great-grandparents were born in Lask?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the population of Lask?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- I know it was 50% Jewish. And the total population could have been someplace, I don't know, 6,000, 7,000. But it was a Jewish town with Jewish organizations-- Zionists from Hashomer Hatzair, which is the left to Betar to [NON-ENGLISH], which is Hashomer Hatzair is, of course, this Communist-- at particular time, there were communists that were leading that organization at that time. But you had everything. You had Maccabi. You had everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Jewish library in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Jewish library. There was a Jewish life. In a little town like that, we had a large synagogue. We had [NON-ENGLISH], I don't even know how many. There was [NON-ENGLISH] Alexander, and others that I don't remember. And there was a [NON-ENGLISH], where the artisans-- like tailors, cap makers, artists like that-- prayed in their own atmosphere. And it was beautiful. It was beautiful. Because as a matter of fact, there were fights going on between the [NON-ENGLISH], who should have them before the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, by the [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was the kind of arguments that used to be there. But it was all in a friendly way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father. First his name, his full name, your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my softest subject. Because I think there wasn't another person in the world that will ever be like my father. And that's not only my opinion. But anyone from our town thought of my father-- there's a Jewish saying, [NON-ENGLISH], which means to god and to the people. My father's name was Herschel Piernakarz. Even the Gentiles, the Poles, liked him. He was an officer in the Polish Army. He was involved in so many charitable endeavors in a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you name them? Could you name what he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, of course. He was on the board of the [NON-ENGLISH], which loaned money without interest to people that needed just to carry over from one season to the other, from one holiday to the other. Or in business-- [INAUDIBLE] when I say business, it's not American business. You know, help a tailor buy some fabric or a shoemaker buy some sole leather, that sort of thing. And for families that just had more bad luck than usual, to borrow some money. My father was involved in a group of Jewish theater amateurs, where they played [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. So many, so many. And the rehearsals were in our shop, so it stayed with me. And the money that they made went for poor people for-- we're talking about Jewish people. The money was going for coal for winter, for matzahs, for poor brides, a little bit of a trousseau, that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said shop. What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a custom tailor. But he employed, at one time, nine people, which was a tremendous amount. At times, it was less. And I remember how I came before the Easter, Christmas-- Christmas too, but mostly Easter, which is the gentile holiday. So we had some Jewish neighbors, people that never worked. They had other kind of businesses where they were so envious that the people were working day and night, that like the wheel is turning, so gold is coming out. People worked-- worked-- my father worked very hard but did everything to keep his family-- to support his family, to see that his wife in the summer could be with the children, away from the house, which was a few kilometers. It wasn't too far, but the children had the fresh air. My father was involved in the Red Cross. He was involved in the Maccabi. He was-- There wasn't an organization in town that he wasn't involved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you say that he had a shop. Was it part of your house or was it separate from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived downstairs on the grand floor. And one flight up was our shop, where my mother never permit me to go up. Her children are not going to be tailors. And that being tailor-- that being tailor that I learned during the war saved my life. You know, when we get to that part, I'll tell you. That being tailor saved my life, and it made me be in the United States, whatever I'm now was with that little bit of tailoring that I learned, you know, at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Rochma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-R-A-N-T, Frant. She was a quiet person, a very good mother and a very good wife and a balabosta. It always-- everything in the house shined.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your house to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, it wasn't as big as the houses I have now or houses that you see in the United States. But for that time, it was elaborate, elaborate. If I slept in the same room, [INAUDIBLE] in the dining room, so that was elaborate. Because some people had only one room. But there was one-- you know, that cleanliness sometimes was going too far. And, God forbid, I don't blame anyone. But the floor was polished with a red color, and it was very slippery. And before I was born, I had a brother that slipped on that, when he was three years old, broke a leg, was taken to Lodz to the hospital. The nurse that was supposed to take care of him took care also of a child that had the measles, and my brother caught the measles. He was laying wet, he wasn't changed. And he never came out of the hospital. So my parents could never forgive themselves for this. So maybe that's why I was so pampered, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one that died? Chaim, but I never-- I never knew him. I never knew him. He died in 1925, and I was born in '26. So-- But-- My mother was-- I can never forget, you know, maybe I'm getting ahead of myself. But that one point where my mother stuffed two deutschmark in my hand during the occupation already. I know I'm getting ahead of myself. But if-- if you'll take out a girl, you'll have two deutschmark. Now there was no place to go, nothing. But being a girl in her time, she knew how important it was for a young men to have two deutschmark or whatever it was, the currency, to have in the pocket. It was a very warm, a very understanding person. But those years, they were, what women thought of was mostly to take care of the children. And my father was you know, the big man of the house. And he took care of us. It so happens that I have practiced-- and I'm not too proud of it-- but I practised the same thing in my marriage, where I never let my wife work. And that was a big mistake. But it was what it was. So my father take care of the family, and he sent us on vacations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go on vacation?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few miles from our hometown. And one time we went from the school on a trip to Warsaw. That was something spend-thrift. I mean very, spend-thrift, yes, because it was expensive. And not many children would-- I don't remember exactly the details of what happened there. Because the war broke out, I was 13. So I could have been 10 at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What elementary school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our time, there were two elementary schools. There was one, a dilapidated building for the Jewish children. As I said before, there were 50 % Jewish people in our town. And another building, a brand new brick building, four-story building for the gentile children. Now we were very crowded, so they give us one floor in that brick building, but it was the top floor. And God forbid if anyone of us was stuck during recess and we were late, going up to our fourth room. And if they caught us on the steps, they would beat us. They would fight with us, the Polish boys, you know. But that's what it was. That was the way of life. Before you went to the cheder or to the [NON-ENGLISH] that I later went to, we had that in the evening. It was the public school was in the morning till 2:00 and 5:00 to 8:00 we had the Jewish school, the cheder. We would never walk alone. When we walked out, there would be 10 of us. And even so, we wouldn't fight them. Because all they-- shortly before the war, it was such a situation that a Polish person could say that we called him a Polish pig, and the parents would be responsible. No questions asked, they would be punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no comfort between the Jews of the town and the Polish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe between the older people, I don't know. Because my father was an officer in the Polish army. Our customers were mostly Polish people. And they had respect for that picture in the uniform that they saw in our house. My father was not afraid to walk in the evening, even though he always walked with a cane. Not that he needed the cane, but it was safer. And the Jewish boys in town were not afraid of the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you fought a lot as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, really not. I wasn't a fighter. Jewish children were told not to fight, to stay out of the fight, stay out of the way, you know, because they were always right. And I know that our customers were the police, the commissioner. And they would warn us of any trouble. Like we saw Tevya-- Tevya, they would come to warn us. And as a matter of fact, the commissioner, if he tried on some uniform in our house, he would pull the curtains, so the priest wouldn't see him. Because what would happen-- and this is not just exaggerate, this is a fact. Because on Sundays, our shop was open, and the people would work. And some of the peasants that would come from the villages would leave their bicycles in our house. So I watched these bicycles. And when they came out from the church and they would come to our house. And they would tell my father the things that were said about the Jews, especially Easter time. And from time to time, there were fights, that they came out and they would attack Jews. They would get drunk. The bars were open. They would come up from the church. They would have some drinks and then [NON-ENGLISH], let's kill the Jew. But these were Jews that weren't so easily killed. You know, these were boys that wouldn't let them, would fight back. So in our town, I don't remember any time where-- except very shortly before the war, when things had changed dramatically for the wars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have another brother besides the one that died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, yes, I had a three-year-old-- a brother three years younger than I. His name was Loeser. But we called him Lolek. Strange thing is this, I can't remember his face. You know, it eats my guts out. He was-- the way I was a quiet boy, he was full of life. Well, you know, full of life you would say by a grown person. But he was more of a lively kid. He was all over. And wherever I went, he would follow and tell parents oh, I spoke to a girl, you know. He was a real good kid. But he was taken away with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he sleep in house, in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We both slept in one bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of you together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I don't know. To be close with a brother was natural. That question, at that time, never came up. You had to be close with a brother, because it's your brother. You know? Today, you're more sophisticated. You're close and you're not close. But of course we were close. You knew it without anyone telling you that you have to take care on your brother, and you've got to take him with you. And we did things together, because only three years-- three years difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever go to the movies in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my-- my father would always give me some money to go to the movies, a matinee, you know, the comics, these cartoons, rather. You know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would you go with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly with my friends, you know? Not all-- not everyone had the 25 grosze to go to the, you know, movie. But if we were good, behaved, you know, we got it. That was such a different kind of childhood than what you see today that it's very difficult for someone that wasn't around at that time to understand how it was. To give you an example, public school-- of course, there were mostly Jewish teachers. It was a Jewish public school. But there were also the gentile teachers. But the Jewish teachers-- there was one teacher, Franka Korn, that was like a mother. Now it wasn't just teachers. You had guardians, teachers, guardians. In other words, whatever you did in another class, they were still the guardian. And shortly before the war-- I had to be 12 years, because when the war broke out I was 13. So that was at least one year before. We had what was called [NON-ENGLISH], which is a tea party. And she would put on a record player and teach us dancing.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was like a mother to us. I remember bringing the hot water from my house, because I lived very close. And she taught me the tango, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same woman, the same woman, we were with her together in Germany after the war. And being we had no parents, when we signed [NON-ENGLISH], engagement, with the rabbi, she was sitting next to me like-- between me and my wife-- like a mother, you know, and signed the document. And also she learned English herself. And as soon as she took the English lessons, she brought in her children, whatever was left in Germany, maybe I don't know, four or five, and she taught us English. She was a real important teacher, [INAUDIBLE] a wonderful person. But what I'm saying, you know, it's completely a different environment. Besides, it was a small town, where everyone knew everyone, from generation to generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about sports? Did you participate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, we had the Maccabi. And we did all the sports, what is usually done in the sports club. In our building, where we lived, was the Stern, which was more a political organization with sports. Stern means the-- stern is a star, which, in a way represents the symbol of the left. So we belonged to Maccabi, which was a Zionist, supposedly non-political sports organization. And you know, Maccabi exists to this very day. And it's so happens that the instructor of this Maccabi had gone to Israel very early in the '30s, and he became the tsar of sport in Israel. His name is Schmuel Bialig. And he was the right hand to the mayor of Haifa. Five minutes ago I knew his name-- [NON-ENGLISH]. Mayor [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the mayor of Haifa. He was the right hand, Schmuel Bialig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play other towns when you played soccer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we played soccer but we didn't other towns. I was mostly more involved in the scouts and the Hanoar Hatzioni, which was the center Zionists. And the children were called Hanoar, which means the youth. We would go to the-- you know, you would have uniforms and would go for these so-called wars, where you tag-- you tag your enemy and you win his tie. So-- And it so happened that in 1939, on August 30 when we came out from one of these excursions, we saw a sign on the wall, mobilization. And we were so happy we were going to see a real war. And we did see a real war, you know. And the very next morning, we had dead people in our-- in our city. Because we were close to it, not far from the German border, but the plane--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what synagogue did your father go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to [NON-ENGLISH]. My grandfather went to that big, large synagogue, where once in a while, I would go with my grandfather, you know. Especially around bar mitzvah time, you know, when he wanted to show off. I was the oldest grandson. I know that the grandparents are very proud of all the grandchildren. But being my father was a tailor, I wore one of those high school uniforms that they wore in Europe, a navy uniform with a hat. And I carried the flag on Polish Independence Day, which was November 11. So going from the place where everyone met in the city square, the Jewish children walk to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we passed my grandfather's house. And I could look in the window, and he was sitting there, so proud. So he gave me later 20 grosze. That was to show how proud he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I tell my grandchildren, every time I see them, how much I love them. My granddaughter told me, the six-year-old told me this yesterday, I said, you know, Jenny, I love you. She says, grandpa, you will always love me. But those exchanges were not had between grandparents at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grandfather's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leibish Mendel Frant. He was a very fine person. In my grandparents' home, my grandmother was the balabosta. She was-- today you would say the boss. But it wasn't that. She was everything. Everyone looked up to her. And what she said, that was it. I don't remember one word what she said. But I remember everything my grandfather said. In his quiet way, there's so many things that I repeat, you know. I even tell my-- sometimes I tell my children or grandchildren, you know, my grandfather said that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grandmother's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Frieda Wysocki. I think she came, probably, from a wealthier, financially, family. [NON-ENGLISH] was a name of a company that-- that was selling tea in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think she came from that family, I am not sure. Because I know there was a certain air about her, you know, that wealth. But they lived very humbly, you know? What did your grandfather do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was making wooden shoes, where he would go to the woods, buy trees. Then they were chopped up in pieces. Then the wooden soles would be made. Then my uncles would buy leather, cut out the top and put wire and nails, these hooks. So the peasants used that to go to the barns. You know, Jewish people didn't wear that. The First World War, he was a tailor. But after the first World War, he gave this up. And that was a pretty good business. I know they lived comfortably. But in those years, comfortable was-- everything's relative.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in the army as well, your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, no. My grandfather used to tell me that the work made uniforms for the tsar in the First World War. And they were told to throw in-- see, in tailor language, you say, why don't you throw in the sleeves first and sew them in. So the work is literally throw in the sleeves without sewing them in in the packages and shipped it to the-- to the tsar. You know, little stories my grandfather told me that I remember today. And I'm not going to quote him here. But quiet man, he did his-- like came Friday, he prepared the pieces of wood for the stove so the Shabbos goy, the one that lights the candle-- the fire, would come in. He cut up little pieces of paper for the toilet paper. He did his duty, prayed three times a day. I can tell you something. I don't know if he was very pious, very religious. But he was a saint. He did what was right, from morning till night, never offended anyone, never cheating. That's completely out of the question. You know, everything had to be right with the children, with the grandchildren. He didn't go for any-- for any nonsense. It had to be right, and there was no in between. There was no in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I get a little older, I realized how much I loved him. Because there was a certain straightness, you know. You knew what to expect, you know? Do you remember a holiday with your family? A special holiday, Pesach, Purim, something that stands out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I remember the holidays. This is the worst part, but I even remember a simple Friday night, and my father's biggest wish was to be able to say grace [NON-ENGLISH], which means you need three people older than bar mitzvah. And he never lived to have that. You know, never lived to have that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you always have someone come from shul for Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they weren't always. It was a small town. But whenever there was, my father would bring him [NON-ENGLISH]. And we always had a chair. My mother expected that my father will bring a guest. And these were wanderers, people that wandered from town to town. And they were always treated better than anyone else in the house, you know. Because to make them feel it was like-- it was tradition or-- no one thought of it as charity. It was something that you felt obliged to do, you know. And you didn't tell the whole world about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite food at the Shabbat table?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lokshn [NON-ENGLISH]. Chicken noodles, that's what-- every Friday night. And my father would get the fat, you know. And that's why he was sick and had to go to a spa. Because now we know why, he always have it in his stomach. Because he would get the [NON-ENGLISH], the head. And from the soup, chicken soup, there had to be those eyes on the soup, because that was the respect for my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
